coredata.dc:title: Formation of carbon quantum dots and graphene nanosheets from different abundant carbonaceous materials
coredata.dc:creator: Saikia, Monikankana
Das, Tonkeswar
Dihingia, Nikalabh
Fan, Xing
Silva, Luis F.O.
Saikia, Binoy K.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene nanosheets (GNs) are one of the most attractive fluorescent carbon nano-sized materials with unique features of optical and physico-chemical properties having various applications in chemical sensing, biomedicine, semiconductor devices, photo-catalyzing, and electrocatalysis. The primary purpose of this preliminary work is the feasibility study for synthesis of nano-structured value-added carbon products i.e., CQDs and GNs from cheap and abundant carbon sources, such as coal, petroleum coke, graphite, and coal-based humic acid by hydrothermal techniques. The physico-chemical properties of the raw precursors were evaluated by proximate and ultimate analysis. High resolution-transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), Raman spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transforms infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD), UV–visible (UV–vis), and fluorescence (FL) spectroscopy were used to achieve physico-chemical parameters of carbon nanostructures, to better comprehend the development of carbon quantum dots and graphene nanosheets. HR-TEM analysis indicates that the dimensions of the synthesized CQDs from different carbon sources exist in the range of 3–6.5 nm. The approximate quantum yields (QY) of the synthesized carbon nanomaterials were found to be about 2–22%. The synthesized carbon based nanomaterials (i.e. CQDs and GNs) have been efficiently used in the photo-degradation of harmful 2-nitrophenols (2-NP) with the efficiency of 80.79% for CQDs and 82.53% for GNs, respectively. The present study provides a simple and efficient technique for large-scale fabrication of typical CQDs and GNs from abundantly available low-grade coal and other carbonaceous materials for photo-catalysis application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbonaceous materials
Carbon dots
Graphene nanosheet
Fluorescent materials
Hydrothermal treatment
Photocatalytic degradation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon nanomaterials (CQDs and GNs)
Observation from FT-IR and XPS analysis
Quantum yields of CQDs and GNs
Photocatalytic activity of CQDs and GNs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM images of CD-1 at 20 nm (a), 10 nm (b), and corresponding particle size distribution indicating the synthesis of 3.2–5.4 nm size carbon nanoparticles (c); HRTEM images of CD-2 at 10 nm (d), 5 nm (e), and corresponding particle size distribution indicating the synthesis of 3.5–6.5 nm sizes of carbon nanoparticles (f); HRTEM images of CD-4 at 20 nm (g), 2 nm (h), and corresponding particle size distribution indicating the synthesis of 4.5–6.5-nm sizes of carbon nanostructure (i); HRTEM images of chemically converted graphene nanosheets of CD-3 at 100 nm (j) and 20 nm (k).

(a) Raman spectra showing the D- and G-bands of the synthesized carbon quantum dots and graphene nanosheets, (b) FT-IR analysis of the synthesized carbon quantum dots and graphene nanosheets showing the presence of functional groups.

(a) XPS survey spectra of CD-1; (b) deconvoluted C1s XPS spectra of CD-1; (c) deconvoluted O1s XPS spectra of CD-1; (d) XPS survey spectra of CD-2; (e) deconvoluted C1s XPS spectra of CD-2; (f) deconvoluted O1s XPS spectra of CD-2; (g) XPS survey spectra of CD-3; (h) deconvoluted C1s XPS spectra of CD-3; (i) deconvoluted O1s XPS spectra of CD-3; (j) XPS survey spectra of CD-4; (k) deconvoluted C1s XPS spectra of CD-4; and (l) deconvoluted O1s XPS spectra of CD-4.
XRD patterns of CD-3 sample (GNs) showing the presence of the plane (002).

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of the synthesized CQDs and graphene nanosheet (GNs) and (b) Fluorescence spectra of the synthesized CQDs and graphene nanosheet (excited at 320 nm); image of Carbon dots under (c) under natural sunlight and (d) under UV-light (at 365 nm) shows bright blue fluorescence. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

UV–vis absorbance spectra of 2-NP degradation in presence of (a) CQDs (representative sample for CD-1, CD-2, CD-4) and (b) CD-3 (GNs) under irradiation of natural sunlight; (c) 2-NP removal performance of CDs and GNs under sunlight irradiation and in dark.
Types of carbonaceous precursor samples used and their reaction conditions.

head.ce:title: Formation of carbon quantum dots and graphene nanosheets from different abundant carbonaceous materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene nanosheets (GNs) are one of the most attractive fluorescent carbon nano-sized materials with unique features of optical and physico-chemical properties having various applications in chemical sensing, biomedicine, semiconductor devices, photo-catalyzing, and electrocatalysis. The primary purpose of this preliminary work is the feasibility study for synthesis of nano-structured value-added carbon products i.e., CQDs and GNs from cheap and abundant carbon sources, such as coal, petroleum coke, graphite, and coal-based humic acid by hydrothermal techniques. The physico-chemical properties of the raw precursors were evaluated by proximate and ultimate analysis. High resolution-transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), Raman spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transforms infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD), UV–visible (UV–vis), and fluorescence (FL) spectroscopy were used to achieve physico-chemical parameters of carbon nanostructures, to better comprehend the development of carbon quantum dots and graphene nanosheets. HR-TEM analysis indicates that the dimensions of the synthesized CQDs from different carbon sources exist in the range of 3–6.5 nm. The approximate quantum yields (QY) of the synthesized carbon nanomaterials were found to be about 2–22%. The synthesized carbon based nanomaterials (i.e. CQDs and GNs) have been efficiently used in the photo-degradation of harmful 2-nitrophenols (2-NP) with the efficiency of 80.79% for CQDs and 82.53% for GNs, respectively. The present study provides a simple and efficient technique for large-scale fabrication of typical CQDs and GNs from abundantly available low-grade coal and other carbonaceous materials for photo-catalysis application.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon is the most abundant and indispensable element present in earth crust in its elemental form as coal, graphite, and diamond. Carbon nanostructures constituting of carbon allotrope have been widely investigated in few decades due to their unique characteristics of hybridization [1,2]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is an important carbon nano-material with dimensions <10 nm, comprising of amorphous or crystalline center with dominating sp2 carbon and an oxidised carboxylic carbon surface [3,4]. Another significant carbon nanomaterial with two-dimensional structures having planes with size <100 nm known as graphene nanosheets (G-nanosheets), which consist of planar sp2-hybridized carbon layer [5,6]. CQDs have gathered immense attention owing to their stable photochemical characteristics, non-toxicity and associated safety to living tissues, significant stability, water solubility, and easy functionality of the surface. CQDs show broad and vital applications in the area of biological labeling, biomedicine, sensing, photo-catalysis, drug delivery, etc. [7,8]. Xu et al. [9] first discovered CQDs in 2004 by preparative electrophoresis during the distillation of single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs). In the current years, its optical features and fluorescent emissions have particularly gained importance among the electronic and physico-chemical features of CQDs.

Currently, various synthetic approaches have been proposed for the fabrication of CQDs from different source materials. Alongside the size development of the executed precursors, CQDs can be produced by two sorts of methodologies: top-down and bottom-up. The synthesis of CQDs from carbon domain such as coal, graphite, carbon black, and activated carbon by arc discharge [9], laser ablation [10,11], electrochemical oxidation [12,13], chemical oxidation [14,15], or ultrasonic synthesis [16,17] are referred to as top-down method. Bottom-up methodologies prepare CQDs from molecular precursors such as glucose, sucrose, and citric acid by thermal decomposition [18,19], hydrothermal treatment [3,20], microwave synthesis [21,22], or plasma treatment [23]. Among them, oxidation has turned out to be the most regularly utilized technique; however, the traditional oxidation procedure to synthesize CQDs commonly utilizes H2SO4/HNO3 and other environmentally-unfriendly strong acids [24,25]. The restricting variables for these operating procedures are of long processing times (24–48 h), necessity of pre-oxidation and slower cleansing technique. In some instances, we find limitations in the quantum yield as well as in the purity of the CQDs. In such situation, it is challenging either to adapt a useful and facile production technique for CQDs with excellent optical characteristics or to identify different economic and abundant sources.

Low-quality/-grade coals remain unattended world-wide due to its non-suitability in power plants. However, its structure includes a large amount of graphitic crystallite carbon domains, chemically bonded by aliphatic and aromatic hydrocarbon [24,26,27]. Such tiny crystalline domains are rich in carbon and satisfy the size of the quantity dots. If a strong chemical approach can be applied to exfoliate these domains, these coals become an optimal source for the formation of high-value carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Other abundant waste and/or abundant carbonaceous materials, such as low-cost petroleum coke by-products, humic acid, and low-grade graphite can be used as a precursor to synthesize these high-value carbon nanomaterials (i.e. here CQDs and GNs). Graphite is among the cheapest and available source for the fabrication of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). To date, only a few articles have indicated that GQDs are prepared using graphite, perhaps owing to the excellent tasks of exfoliating and breaking tightly packed graphite layers into tiny nanometer dots [28]. Wu et al. [29] established that petroleum coke, an oil-refining by-product, was confirmed to be an excellent precursor for porous carbon production. Shao et al. [30] also described that petroleum coke possessing benzene or aromatic domains, includes graphitic framework, allows synthesis of CQDs by chemical oxidation process. Shi et al. [31] reported that, humic acid is a good precursor for the fabrication of graphene quantum dots via easy and simple hydrothermal treatment. Dong et al. [32] synthesized carbon dots from humic acid by chemical reduction method. It can be noted that humic acid, an amorphous micro-organic compounds that possess numerous benzene rings with conjugated bonds and rings with different attached functional groups may be utilized as a precursor for the fabrication of CQDs.

Carbon nanomaterials have been used in diverse field of applications. Atchudan et al. utilized Phyllanthus acidus (P.acidus) and NH3 (aq) as the carbon and nitrogen sources in order to synthesize fluorescent N-doped C-dots for the detection of Fe3+, and to use N-doped carbon dots (CDs) for the purpose of cell imaging and as fluorescent ink [33]. Moreover, the multicolor in-vivo cell-imaging in Caenorhabditis elegans (C. elegans) was examined with the N-doped CDs by Atchudan et al. [34]. N-doped CDs were synthesized by using betel leaf as a C and N source and it was used as a nano-probe for identifying Fe3+ ion. It was also used for the imaging of colon cancer cells of human [35]. Further, glucose concentrations can be identified by using N-doped carbon-sheets enclosed TiO2 nanoparticles which act as a photochemical biosensor [36]. In addition, S and N rich carbon sheets and N-doped carbon nanograss can be used as an electroactive material for high-efficiency supercapacitors and electrocatalyst for hydrogen evolution reaction [37,38]. The chemically converted graphene-like nanosheets/graphene nanocomposites were also found to be an effective photocatalyst for the degradation of harmful 2-nitrophenols [39,40].

Hereby, we describe a single-step hydrothermal technique for the synthesis of CQDs from subbituminous Northeast Indian coal, petroleum coke, and coal-derived humic acid. This manuscript also describes the formation of chemically converted G-nanosheets (GNs) from commercially available graphite powder by simple hydrothermal technique. Thus, the present preliminary work provides the use of new and alternative low-cost abundant carbonaceous material for the fabrication of blue-fluorescent CQDs/GNs by an easy and simple technique. This study also opens up a new avenue for large-scale production of high-impact low-cost CQDs and GNs.

The worth mentioning novelty of this study is the use of abundant source materials including low-grade coal, petroleum coke, and coal-derived humic acid as the cheap precursors to synthesize CQDs and GNs. The novelty also depends on the point that the current technique overwhelms the aforesaid inconveniences, including reaction time and eco-friendliness. Furthermore, the study establishes a beneficial and easy technique to synthesize CQDs and chemically converted G-nanosheets from these sources without using any hazardous chemicals.

In current times, carbon nanomaterials have been used as an innovative material to mitigate organic pollutants from contaminated water, which has now turned into one of the worst environmental problems. Among all the contaminants, nitrophenol and its derivatives play a critical role for adverse health conditions. Due to their high toxicity, nitrophenols are considered as the main organic contaminants in water bodies, as they are largely used in dyestuffs, fertilizer, and pharmaceutical manufacturing industries [39]. Due to its resistance to bio-chemical degradations, nitrophenol polluted water is challenging to purify. Consequently, there is an urgent need in different industries for the progress of an economic cost-efficient procedure for reducing nitrophenol concentration together with other organic contaminants. In the current study, the synthesized CQDs and GNs were satisfactorily used as active photocatalyst for the removal/degradation of toxic 2-nitrophenol from water solution.

The low-quality subbituminous coal sample (referred to as Coal-NG) was obtained from Northeastern (NER) coalfields of India. Petroleum Coke (referred to as PC) used in the study was obtained from a petroleum refinery in the Northeastern region of India. The samples were air-dried, crushed, and then sieved by using standard methods to sizes <0.211-mm [41]. Low-quality coal-derived humic acid (referred as Coal-NK-HA) and commercially purchased graphite (denoted as CG) were also used for experimental purposes. To obtain the desired CQDs products, the samples were passed through the ultrafiltration system (KrosFlo TFF system; SYR2-U10-A; Spectrum). In accordance with the procedure suggested by Das et al., 2015 [42] humic acid was extracted from the NER coal sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon nanomaterials (CQDs and GNs)
ce:section.ce:para: 60 mg of the grounded samples were dissolved in 60 mL of ultrapure water in a beaker. The blend was then shifted without further agitation to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. The reactor was then placed in a furnace and kept at a temperature of 200 °C for 120 min. Following air cooling, the pH was measured and the blend was passed through a 0.22-μm membrane and further filtered by using dialysis membrane/ultrafiltration kit. As we know that, the precursors utilized to synthesize CQDs and GNs may possess or may be contaminated by impurities, the purification of the synthesized carbon nanomaterials was done in order to isolate the CQDs and GNs from the side-products through ultrafiltration (KrosFlo TFF system; SYR2-U10-A; Spectrum). Moreover, it is to be mentioned that the small molecular fluorophores produced during the synthesis of CQDs also produces blue-fluorescence emission. In order to separate the smaller molecular fluorophores from the larger particles or to purify from smaller contaminates, we had dialyzed the solutions in a 1 kDa dialysis bag (Sigma-Aldrich) for 5 days to get the products. After dialysis, the carbon nanomaterials (i.e. CQDs and GNs) were achieved by further concentrating the filtrate using rotary evaporator. Table 1 summarizes the types of carbonaceous samples and their reaction conditions for production of CQDs and GNs. The synthesized products are designated as CD-1, CD-2, CD-3, and CD-4 in the subsequent discussion.

Proximate and ultimate analyses of the raw carbonaceous materials were performed as per standard ASTM methods (ASTM D7582-15, ASTM D3176-15) [43,44]. TEM/HR-TEM analysis was performed to evaluate the morphology/microstructure of CQDs produced by adopting the techniques reported in our earlier studies [45–47]. The functional groups present in fabricated CQDs were determined by the FT-IR spectra (System-2000, Perkin-Elmer). The X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Model: ESCALAB Xi+) was used to evaluate the nature of carbon bonding. The Raman analysis was carried out by a Laser micro-Raman system with a laser excitation wavelength at 532 nm (Model: LabRam HR) to determine the degree of graphitization following procedure adopted elsewhere [47]. The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) carried out in a Rigaku X-ray diffractometer by scanning at a rate of 3o/min from 5 to 90o. The photo-physical analyses (UV–vis and Fluorescence) were done by the procedure as mentioned earlier [45,47].
The quantum yield of the synthesized carbon nanomaterials was determined by using the following equation:

where, “Φ” indicates relative quantum yield with respect to reference, “I” refers to the integrated emission intensity, “η” indicates the refractive index of solvent, and “A” denotes absorbance. The subscript “r” indicates the standard index of the quinine sulfate. The detailed procedure adopted for determination of quantum yields of the produced CQDs as outlined in our earlier reports [45,47].

The degradation study was done by using 2-nitrophenol (2-NP) as an ideal organic pollutant to estimate the photocatalytic efficiency of the synthesized carbon nanomaterials under natural sunlight. The three CQDs sample (CD-1, CD-2, and CD-4) were equivalently mixed to form a representative sample and the G-nanosheets (CD-3) were used as the photocatalyst for the degradation activity. Both the experiments were performed between 10:00 and 14:00 h in bright sunny days. Typically, 30 mL aqueous suspension of 0.1 mM concentration of 2-NP and a desired amount of photocatalyst were mixed together and stirred in dark for about 120 min to assure adsorption/desorption equilibrium and then the mixture was exposed to sunlight for definite time period. 2 mL of each reaction mixture was extracted during the experiment at various time intervals and each specimen was separated by a membrane filter. By using UV–vis spectrophotometer (Model: LABINDIA 1000+), the residual phenolic concentrations along with were calculated by measuring the absorbance of the filtrates. The rate of photocatalytic degradation of both the samples was calculated by the following equation.
Degradation Efficiency
%
=
1 −
C t
/
C o
× 100 %
where, Co and Ct are the initial and final concentrations of 2-NP. In the absence of a photocatalyst, blank studies have also been executed with 2-nitrophenol solutions.

The physico-chemical characteristics of Coal-NG, coal-based humic acid (Coal-NK-HA), petroleum coke (PC), and commercial graphite (CG) are summarized in Table 2 . All the carbon based materials revealed the presence of higher quantities of carbon, together with lower quantities of hydrogen and nitrogen. The chemical assessment showed that the carbon samples have a difference in their composition of carbon (50–70%), hydrogen (5–7%), and ash contents (0.60–4%). The carbon content is highest in the petroleum coke. Coal-NG and Coal-NK-HA have higher volatile matters than the rest of the raw samples.

The TEM/HRTEM analysis portrayed the morphological structure, composition, and microstructure of CQDs produced from the carbonaceous precursors. This study also helps to specify the size distribution of the CQDs [48]. Fig. 1 (a,d,g) depicts the images of CD-1,CD-2, and CD-4, which revealed that synthesized CQDs are homogeneous, globular in shape, and are uniformly distributed without aggregation. Fig. 1(b,e,h) shows the HR-TEM images of the synthesized CQDs indicating its crystallinity. The size distribution histograms are shown in Fig. 1(c,f,i) signifying the synthesized nanostructures are in the range of 3.2–6.5 nm in diameter. The form and size of the obtained CQDs are similar to the results described elsewhere [24,49–51]. Fig. 1(j) shows the presence of chemically converted graphene-like nanosheets (GNs) in CD-3 sample. The HR-TEM image of the graphene nanosheets is also demonstrated in the Fig. 1(k). The sheets were usually found to be twisted or overlapped in some locations and were similar to the graphene nanosheets (GNs) observed earlier [5,6]. The number of layers can be examined through an assessment of its twisted areas at greater magnification [52]. To further analyze the arrangement, as well as to access the features of the synthesized CQDs and GNs, other advance characterization techniques were carried out as described.

Raman spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 2 (a), for CQDs (CD-1 and CD-4) demonstrates three distinct bands at 1328 cm−1, 1435 cm−1for D-band and 1623 cm−1for G-band and at 1317 cm−1, 1440 cm−1 for D-band and 1612 cm−1 for G-band, respectively. The initial peaks ranging from about 1317 to 1328 cm−1 indicates the D-band for sp3 hybridized carbon [53]. The second peak in the range of 1435 to 1440 cm−1 also refers to be D-band representing the sp3 hybridized carbon structures [54]. The peaks at about 1612 and 1623 cm−1 indicate the G-band corresponding to the sp2 carbon structures [55]. CD-2 and CD-3 showed the characteristic G-band at 1629 cm−1 and 1634 cm−1, respectively, that matched with disordered carbon and sp2-hybridized carbon framework, indicating the presence of aromatic groups inside carbon nanomaterials. However, two bands were observed at about 1294 cm−1 in CD-3 and at 1317 cm−1 in CD-2 with very low amplitude. The hybridized sp2‑carbon structure corresponds to G-band and D-band generated due to lattice defects possessing the sp3-hybridized carbon [56,57]. Based on the intensity of G-peak, the synthesized carbon nanomaterials were chiefly graphitic in nature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Observation from FT-IR and XPS analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 (b) shows the FTIR spectra of the synthesized CQDs and GNs, which shows the existence of CC, CO, CO, HC, and OH vibrational frequencies. The broad peak observed nearby at 3400 cm−1 is due to OH bond stretch and the existence of COOH group [45]. The band absorbed at around 2850 cm−1 and 2930 cm−1 is observed as a result of stretching symmetric and asymmetric vibrational modes of CH3 and CH2 [46,58]. The bands within the range of 1600–1650 cm−1 signify the stretching of CO and CC bonds [6]. Around 1400 cm−1, the synthesized nanomaterials possess definite peaks due to CH vibrational mode and the existence of a sharp peak at 1100 cm−1 indicates the presence of CO bond in the carbon nanoproducts. It is observed that the spectra of CD-4 and CD-2 are slightly broader in comparison to CD-1 and CD-3 spectra which may be due to the presence of some amount of moisture in it. As such, we can conclude that CQDs and GNs possess aromatic hydroxyl functional groups [46,59]. Considering the existence of these oxygen-containing functional groups, the synthesized CQDs and graphene nanosheets are water-soluble.

As displayed in Fig. 3 , the XPS spectra of the synthesized carbon nanomaterials demonstrated the presence of peaks at 284 eV and 532 eV in the survey spectra, indicating the binding energy of C1s and O1s, respectively. The de-convoluted C1s and O1s XPS spectra of the carbon nanoproducts are shown in Fig. 4 . It shows major peaks at 283.6 eV and 285.5 eV corresponding to CC sp2 hybridized and CO group for CD-1. Fig. 3(e) and Fig. 3(h) show peaks at 283.7 eV and 287.7 eV and 283.5 eV and 287.6 eV, that correlates with the CC (sp2) and CO group for CD-2 and CD-3, respectively. For CD-4, two peaks located at 283.7 eV and 287.4 eV corresponds to CC (sp2) and COH groups which are shown in Fig. 3(k) [60]. For all the carbon nanoproducts synthesized, the O1s XPS peak at 531.5 eV, 530.6 eV, and 531.1 eV, representing organic CO, was observed in Fig. 3(c,f,i,j) [48]. It is observed that the synthesized carbon nanoproducts possess functional groups that are rich in oxygen (e.g. carbonyl, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups) and aromatic structural fragments with sp2-hybridization [50]. So, the synthesized carbon nanoproducts are assumed to be water-soluble.

Crystallinity of the synthesized GNs was studied by XRD analysis as shown in Fig. 4. The resulting chemically converted graphene nanosheets have a framework between the amorphous and the crystalline structures. The XRD pattern of GNs indicates sharp intense broad peak positioned at 2θ of 26.48 (d ~ 3.363 Å) indicating the graphitic crystal plane (002) [46]. The peak observed represents the conjugate sp2 carbon structure and is associated with the aromatic ring structure [49]. The peak observed is close to the graphite peak i.e. 26.4o which might shift slightly from the original graphite peak may be due to the existence of some amorphous carbon framework and oxygen containing functional groups. The degree of graphitization (G) was measured from the XRD pattern by using the Franklin equation [61,62] as mentioned below:

where, d002 is the interlayer spacing of the plane (002) calculated from XRD pattern. The degree of graphitization/crystallinity of the chemically converted graphene nanosheets is found to be around 89%, which is similar in comparison to other synthesized graphite samples [61,62].

The photo-physical characteristics of carbon nanoproducts synthesized were examined by UV–vis and FL spectroscopy. It is revealed that under UV light of 365 nm wavelengths, the CQDs and GNs show blue fluorescence as displayed in Fig. 5d. In order to recognize the existence of pi-domains and optically sensitive deformity points, the photo-physical features of the carbon nanomaterials were further examined by UV–vis spectroscopy.

The final products (CD-1, CD-2, CD-3, and CD-4) after the hydrothermal treatment were analyzed by the UV–vis spectroscopy. Fig. 5(a) represents the UV–vis spectra of the final products. The bands appeared at approximately 250–270 nm are caused by the aromatic π-system where the π-electrons are excited. The transition of n-π* electrons of the oxygen containing functional groups is observed owing to the tailing band at around 300 nm [47]. From the spectra, it was observed that the spectra vary for different samples, signifying varied size and surface functionalized carbon nanomaterials. The UV–vis spectra are in accordance with the absorbance spectra of the carbon nanomaterials, as reported earlier [45,46,50].

Fig. 5(b) represents the FL emission spectra of the CQDs and GNs excited at 320 nm. Among all the samples, CD-4 shows the maximum fluorescence emission at 450 nm signifying the blue fluorescence. It is due to the fact that the OH group has higher intensity in comparison to that of CO group [63]. The n-π* transitions linked with the non-bonding orbitals of carboxylic groups. The mechanism of the fluorescence emission from CQDs and GNs is primarily attributed to the origin of fluorescence dependency on excitation. The variation of the synthetic methods used and structures of CQDs and GNs largely depend on the emission mechanism. With this view, the key objective would be to distinguish the impact of quantum confinement effects that may be related to the presence of π-domains of the conjugated carbon centre, surface states that may be due to the existence of functional groups associated with the carbon frameworks and molecular states in which emission may arise from fluorescent free or bonded molecules. One can assume that due to their tiny sizes, the emission may arise from quantum confinement effect of the conjugated π-domains. The different particle sizes or different functional groups existing on the surface of the carbon nanoproducts determine its FL properties [12]. Moreover, such blue-emission was also suggested by Sciortino et al. [64] which may be due to the tiny organic fluorescent molecules, which are connected to the surfaces of the carbon nanomaterials, not exactly to the dot itself.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum yields of CQDs and GNs
ce:section.ce:para: The calculated fluorescence quantum yields (QY) of the synthesized carbon nanoproducts i.e., CD-1, CD-2, CD-3, and CD-4 were bound to be 21.95%, 10.91%, 2.34%, and 2.38%, respectively. Coal-derived CQDs i.e., CD-1 shows the prominent quantum yield among all carbon nanomaterials synthesized from different carbonaceous sources. The QY for the carbon dots fabricated from petroleum coke by hydrothermal treatment was found to be 10.91%, which is observed to be higher than the QY of CQDs synthesized through ultrasonic-assisted chemical oxidation process reported elsewhere [51]. Shi et al. [31] reported that the QY of graphene quantum dots synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of pure humic acid was 5.2% whereas, the yield of CQDs obtained by hydrothermal treatment of coal-derived humic acid is found to 2.28% in the present study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic activity of CQDs and GNs
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, a preliminary test was done in dark for about 2 h for adsorption of 2-NP in order to achieve the adsorption-desorption equilibrium before exposure to sunlight for photocatalysis activity. After that, the reaction mixture was exposed to sunlight under appropriate conditions. The reaction mixtures were extracted and observed at definite time intervals using a UV–vis spectrophotometer. The photocatalytic activity of the synthesized carbon nanomaterials (CQDs and GNs) were estimated by degradation of 2-NP in aqueous conditions under sunlight. The photocatalytic degradation of 2-NP in the presence of CQDs and GNs as photocatalyst shifts the UV–vis absorbance spectra as shown in the Fig. 6(a) and Fig. 6(b) respectively. Under sunlight, degradation efficiency of 2-NP was 80.79% for CQDs and 82.53% for GNs at about 120 min as shown in Fig. 6(c). The degradation efficiency is observed to be more for GNs in comparison to the CQDs. It is seen that 2-NP possesses characteristics peaks at about 275 and 349 nm which progressively drops on exposure to sunlight. Phenols are oxidised during photocatalysis to form hydroxyl phenols by the oxidizing species generated. In dark, the rate of degradation for 0.1 mM concentration of 2-NP was found to be 26.15% and 15.59% for CQDs and GNs, respectively for about 120 min as shown in the Fig. 6(c). This insignificant degradation was observed may be due to the existence of pores and surface chemical groups.

Based on the experimental results, a mechanism has been proposed for the photocatalytic degradation activity of 2-NP. The photocatalytic activity of 2-NP associates with the efficient separation and transfer of photo-initiated charges generated through sunlight. Due to the absorption of sunlight, electrons are excited from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB) which results in the formation of holes (h+) in the CB and electrons (e−) in the corresponding VB. The 2-NP serves as an activating agent under sunlight and the exciting electrons are deposited on surfaces of the synthesized carbon nanomaterial (CQDs and GNs). In turn, CQDs and GNs behave as reducing and oxidizing agent in this degradation process. Both the carbon based nanoproducts (CQDs and GNs) improved the transfer of electron to its surface from 2-NP. In aqueous medium, h+ generates hydroxyl (OH − ) radicals. The adsorbed O2 on the surface traps electron that leads to the development of O 2
− radicals which further reacts with H2O to form extremely active OH radicals. These reactive species (h+, O 2
− , OH) are responsible for the break-down of 2-NP. The following equations are summarized for the possible mechanism regarding the photo-degradation of 2-NP by using the as-synthesized carbon nanomaterials:

Moreover, the CN bonds of 2-NP molecules are oxidised and gets immediately transformed into harmless inorganic molecules NO3
− anions, CO2 and H2O [39,40]. Therefore, it can be assumed that the synthesized CQDs and GNs have the promising potential for photocatalytic degradation of 2-NP into harmless NO3
− anions, CO2, and H2O molecule.

In summary, the hydrothermal technique is proven to involve minimum effort and is a cost effective way of synthesizing of carbon nanomaterials like carbon quantum dots and graphene nanosheets with excellent blue-fluorescent properties (under UV light at 365 nm) from different abundantly available carbonaceous materials. The CQDs and graphene nanosheets produced by this process contain functional groups such as CC, CO, and CO. As confirmed by FTIR and XPS analyses, the existence of the functional groups possessing oxygen signifies that the synthesized CQDs and GNs are water soluble. The technique to transform cheap carbonaceous raw material into environmental-friendly, high-valuable carbon quantum dots and graphene nanosheet can be regarded as an alternative to other complex and expensive processes.

The synthesized carbon nanomaterials (i.e. CQDs and GNs) might also be efficiently used in the photo-degradation of harmful 2-nitrophenols in water systems. However, the optimizations of process parameters are essential for final application of this process in industrial scale.

Authors are much grateful to the Director (CSIR-NEIST) for his encouragements to this futuristic project. The financial grant received from CSIR is highly acknowledged by the authors (MLP-1006). The authors thank Debabrata Gogoi for his analytical assistance in characterization of few samples. Authors are thankful to the reviewers for their constructive comments to improve the paper.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
